What current-limiting resistor do I need for a standard 5 mm red LED driven by a 3.3 V GPIO? Target 10 mA.

LED forward voltage Vf for a red 5 mm is ~2.0 V. Resistor `R = (Vsupply - Vf) / I = (3.3 - 2.0) / 0.010 = 130 ohms`. Round up to the nearest standard E12 value: 150 ohms. Power dissipation `P = I^2 * R = 0.010^2 * 150 = 15 mW`, so a 1/8 W resistor is fine. For green/yellow Vf is ~2.1 V; for blue/white Vf is ~3.0 V which is too close to 3.3 V to drive cleanly, use a boost or higher rail.

I want to read a 12 V car battery on a 3.3 V ADC. Design the divider.

Pick a divider that maps 14.4 V (full alternator) to no more than 3.0 V at the ADC. Ratio = 3.0 / 14.4 = 0.208. Use R1 = 100 k, R2 = 27 k. Vout at 14.4 V = 14.4 * 27 / (100 + 27) = 3.06 V. At nominal 12.6 V you read 2.68 V.

Notes:
1. Keep the divider impedance moderate so ADC sample-and-hold settles. A 0.1 uF cap from the ADC node to GND helps; with R_thev ~21 k and 0.1 uF tau is 2.1 ms.
2. Add a Schottky clamp (BAT54S to GND/VCC) to protect the pin from load-dump spikes.
3. Calibrate by reading the divider at a known voltage; log the offset.

What pull-up resistor value should I use for I2C on a 3.3 V bus with two devices and short wires?

Standard answer: 4.7 k. For 100 kHz SCL with a few hundred pF of bus capacitance, 4.7 k gives a rise time well under the 1 us spec. For 400 kHz Fast-mode use 2.2 k. For 1 MHz Fast-mode-plus use 1.0 k or active pull-ups. For long wires (more than 1 m) drop to 1.0 k and consider a buffer like the PCA9517.

When do I use a pull-down vs a pull-up on a digital input?

Use whichever your switch shorts to the OPPOSITE rail. If a button shorts to GND when pressed, use a pull-up so the line idles HIGH and pulses LOW. If a button shorts to VCC, use a pull-down so the line idles LOW and pulses HIGH. Most MCUs have built-in pull-ups but rarely pull-downs (ESP32 has both). Default to pull-ups + button-to-GND because internal pull-ups exist on every chip.

Compute the RC time constant for a 100 ohm + 100 nF debounce on a tactile switch. Is that enough?

Tau = R * C = 100 * 100e-9 = 10 us. That's far too short. Tactile switch contact bounce is typically 1-5 ms. Use R = 10 k and C = 100 nF for tau = 1 ms, or R = 10 k and C = 1 uF for tau = 10 ms. Pair the RC with a Schmitt-trigger input (most MCU GPIO has Schmitt) so the noisy rising edge becomes a clean digital transition. For a software-only debounce, sample every 5 ms and require 3 stable reads.

Why do datasheets recommend a 0.1 uF AND a 10 uF capacitor next to every IC?

Different jobs. The 0.1 uF (ceramic, X7R) sinks high-frequency current spikes from the IC's own switching. Place it under 5 mm from the VCC pin, ideally on the underside of the board across the via. The 10 uF (ceramic or tantalum) reservoirs charge for slower transients (10s of MHz down to kHz). Together they keep the supply impedance low across the whole spectrum. For a microcontroller add 1 nF too, in parallel.

I have a 12-bit ADC at 3.3 V reference. What is the smallest signal I can resolve?

Resolution = 3.3 / 2^12 = 3.3 / 4096 = 0.806 mV per LSB. That's the theoretical minimum. In practice noise (thermal, switching, EMI) eats 1-3 LSB even with good layout, so reliable resolution is 2-5 mV. To do better, lower Vref (e.g. 1.1 V internal on some chips), oversample (4x oversampling adds 1 effective bit, 16x adds 2), or use an external 16/24-bit ADC like the ADS1115 or ADS1256.

I have a 5 V Arduino TX line that needs to talk to a 3.3 V ESP32 RX. What's the simplest level shift?

Drop in a single 1k + 2k voltage divider on the line: Vin/in_5V to a 1k, then 2k to GND, ESP32 RX taps the junction. 5 V * 2 / (1+2) = 3.33 V at the RX. This works for the 5V-to-3.3V direction at any UART baud. The 3.3V-to-5V direction is fine without translation because 3.3 V exceeds the Arduino's V_IH min (about 0.6 * VCC = 3.0 V at 5 V). For bidirectional I2C use a BSS138 + 10k pair on each line.

What causes a ground loop and how do I fix it?

A ground loop is two paths between the same nominal ground at different potentials, which lets current flow through your signal ground. Causes: long shared returns, multiple chassis grounds, USB host on one supply and sensor on another. Symptoms: hum on audio, varying ADC offset, intermittent comm errors. Fixes: single-point ground (star ground from the main supply), galvanic isolation (digital isolator like the ADuM1201, or optoisolator), or differential signaling (RS485, CAN). For host-to-device USB, plug both into the same outlet strip.

What's the relationship between PWM duty cycle and frequency for driving a brushed DC motor?

Duty cycle controls speed (effective average voltage). Frequency must be above the audible range (above 20 kHz) to avoid whining, but below the motor's electrical bandwidth (typically tens of kHz for hobby motors). Hobby DC motors run well at 20-25 kHz PWM. ESP32 LEDC at 25 kHz with 10-bit resolution is a clean default. Avoid 1 kHz: it whines. Avoid 100 kHz: switching losses in a low-side MOSFET get bad without a proper gate driver.

How do I size a battery voltage divider so it doesn't drain my battery?

Pick R_total so the divider draws under 1 percent of your sleep current. For a 100 uA average sleep current, that's 1 uA through the divider, so R_total > 12 V / 1 uA = 12 Mohm. Use 10 M + 1 M for a 12 V battery. The high impedance breaks ADC sample-and-hold; add a 100 nF cap from the tap to GND and either oversample or buffer with an op-amp follower (TLV9061 is rail-to-rail, 50 nA). Cheaper trick: gate the divider with a P-MOSFET that you only turn on for the read.

Explain open-collector / open-drain.

An open-collector (BJT) or open-drain (MOSFET) output can only sink current. It cannot drive HIGH; HIGH is provided by a pull-up resistor on the line. Multiple devices can share the line: any of them pulling LOW wins. That's how I2C, OneWire, and interrupt-with-shared-pin work. For a simple level shift, an open-drain output on a 3.3 V chip can drive a 5 V line if you pull up to 5 V (ensure the pin is 5 V tolerant in OD mode).

Schmitt trigger vs comparator: which do I want for a slow analog signal crossing a threshold?

If you need adjustable hysteresis or precise threshold, use a comparator (LM393, TLV3201) with positive feedback to set hysteresis explicitly. If you just want digital recovery from a slow/noisy edge into a fixed-threshold logic input, route through a Schmitt-trigger gate (74HC14) which has built-in ~0.4*VCC hysteresis. Most MCU GPIO inputs are already Schmitt; you do not need a discrete one for clean digital signals.

When should I worry about antenna ground-plane requirements on a custom PCB?

Whenever you put a chip antenna or a PIFA trace down. WiFi (2.4 / 5 GHz) and BLE want a solid ground plane at least the size of a quarter-wave (~30 mm at 2.4 GHz) directly beneath and beside the antenna. Cut the ground plane back from under the radiating element itself by the manufacturer's keepout (typically 5-8 mm). Skipping this is the most common reason for poor range on hand-rolled ESP32/nRF52 boards. Module-style chips (ESP32-WROOM, MFB52840) include the keepout in their datasheet drawing.

How do I read a sub-1 mV signal from a thermocouple cleanly?

Don't drive a thermocouple straight into an MCU ADC; the noise floor is bigger than the signal (50 uV/K for K-type). Use a dedicated thermocouple amplifier: MAX31855 (SPI, has cold-junction compensation, K-type only), MAX31856 (programmable type), or AD8495 (analog out, 5 mV/C scaling). Keep the cold junction at the IC, not at the connector. Twist the thermocouple lead pair, route differentially, ground only at the amp.

What's the difference between a polarized aluminum electrolytic and a ceramic capacitor for decoupling?

Aluminum electrolytics: high capacitance per dollar (10-1000 uF), but high ESR (0.1-1 ohm), polarized, age and dry out. Use them for bulk reservoir on the input of a regulator or motor power. Ceramics (X7R, X5R): lower ESR (mohms), no polarity, stable. Use them next to every IC for high-frequency decoupling. Tantalums sit in between (low ESR, polarized, solid). Modern designs go full ceramic plus one large tantalum or polymer cap; pure aluminum is rare on signal boards now.

What does ESR mean for a capacitor and when does it matter?

Equivalent Series Resistance: the resistive part in series with the ideal cap. It limits how fast the cap can dump or absorb current. For a 1 A switching transient, a 100 mohm ESR cap drops 100 mV across itself; a 5 mohm cap drops 5 mV. For LDO output stability, some LDOs require a minimum ESR (e.g. 0.1-1 ohm) and ceramics will make them oscillate; others (modern LDOs like TPS7A/MIC5219) explicitly want low-ESR ceramic. Read the LDO datasheet's stability section.
